coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots with strong nonlinear optical response
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Yamada, Yuya
Zhou, Shifeng
Shimotsuma, Yasuhiko
Miura, Kiyotaka
Qiu, Jianrong
coredata.dc:description: The carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized by a simple method of femtosecond laser ablation of bagasse in ethanol. Strong optical limiting effects of as-prepared CNDs to 800nm femtosecond laser pulses were observed with the threshold of 74mJ/cm2. The strong two photon absorption of CNDs is responsible for the optical limiting response. The nonlinear coefficient was determined by the open-aperture Z-scan technique.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The typical TEM image of CNDs. Inset: (A) HRTEM image, (B) Size distribution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Emission spectrum of CNDs. Inset: Fluorescent images of CNDs drops taken under the excitation of UV, blue, and green light (from top to bottom). (B) Emission spectrum of CNDs excited by the femtosecond laser. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Open aperture Z-scan results of CNDs under input fluences of 22, 37 and 72mJ/cm2. (B) Optical limiting response of CNDs under different input fluences. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Effective two photon absorption coefficients (β) at different excitation fluence.

simple-head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots with strong nonlinear optical response

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence Materials and Devices and the Institute of Optical Communication Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Silicon Materials, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310027, PR China. Fax: +86 57188925079.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized by a simple method of femtosecond laser ablation of bagasse in ethanol. Strong optical limiting effects of as-prepared CNDs to 800nm femtosecond laser pulses were observed with the threshold of 74mJ/cm2. The strong two photon absorption of CNDs is responsible for the optical limiting response. The nonlinear coefficient was determined by the open-aperture Z-scan technique.

ce:sections.ce:para: Luminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the size in the range 1–10nm stand for a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials and have attracted tremendous interest due to their unique properties [1]. CNDs with tunable band gap, stable photoluminescence (PL), easy functionality, excellent biocompatibility, and lower cytotoxicity offer excellent potential candidates for various applications [1,2]. Unfortunately, though extensive efforts have been devoted to the synthesis and optical property investigations of CNDs, the research of the nonlinear optical (NLO) response to ultra-fast laser pulses has nearly been neglected, which limits the scope of further progress in the field of CNDs [3–5]. The study of NLO response of CNDs to femtosecond (fs) pulses is vital for the applications of CNDs in the ultrafast optoelectronic.

As benchmarks, carbon materials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, carbon black and graphene, have been reported to exhibit strong optical limiting response [6–9]. With increasing use of powerful pulsed lasers (e.g. fs laser) for various applications, NLO limiters are significant for pulse shaping, mode locking, and the protection of eyes and sensitive optical devices [7,10]. However, due to slow formation of microbubbles, there are less NLO limiting materials for fs laser pulses than those for nanosecond laser pulses [10,11]. Therefore, it is still necessary and a challenge to establish new and strong optical limiters with fast response and a low threshold for fs laser pulses. In the present work, we establish a new carbon optical limiter, CNDs, to fs laser pulses with a low limiting threshold.

Sugarcane bagasse is the residue produced in large quantities after sugar juice extraction from sugarcane stalks. Herein, CNDs are synthesized by fs laser (800nm, 1kHz and 100fs) ablation of carbonizated sugarcane bagasse powders in ethanol with the energy of 0.5mJ/pulse [2]. Fig. 1 shows a typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CNDs with the average diameter size of 5.3nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image demonstrates crystalline structure of one typical carbon nanodot, as shown in the inset (A) of Fig. 1.

CNDs emit colorful PL, which is dependent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 A). The maximum emission intensity is observed at 393nm using an excitation wavelength of 340nm. Colorful PL is also observed from the fluorescence images of CNDs excited by ultraviolet, blue and green light (inset of Fig. 2A). The quantum yield of as-prepared CNDs is determined to be around 2%. Previous reports suggested that the excitation-wavelength-dependent emission may originate from radiative recombination of excitons through transitions between highly localized defect energy states [1,2]. These prepared CNDs also emit multiphoton (MP) luminescence, when excited by the fs laser with a wavelength of 800nm (Fig. 2B). The previous report implies that the MP luminescence originates from two-photon emission [2].

NLO properties of the CNDs are characterized by using an open-aperture fs laser Z-scan technique [12]. Fig. 3 A shows the open-aperture Z-scan results under different input fluences. Reverse saturable absorption is observed in all three Z-scan curves, which clearly indicate the fs optical limiting effects. With the increase of input fluence, the valley value of transmittance decreases significantly, implying the enhancement of optical limiting effects. The fs optical limiting response is extracted from Z-scan curves (Fig. 3B). The optical limiting threshold (F50, defined as the incident fluence at which the transmittance decreases to 50% of the linear transmittance) is about 74mJ/cm2. We have measured the nonlinear transmission of pure solvent (ethanol, 99.5%) under the same conditions, which show much smaller nonlinearity compared to that of the CNDs solution, suggesting that the major contribution arises from the CNDs. The F50 of the CNDs to fs laser pulses is comparable or smaller than that of various materials (Table S1) [10,11].

Nonlinear absorption, including MP absorption and excited state absorption, is generally proposed to be responsible for the optical limiting effects for fs laser pulses [7,10,11]. To identify the mechanism of the nonlinear absorption and investigate the NLO properties further, quantitative estimate of the nonlinear susceptibility is conducted, according to the experimental methodology and data analyses given in the previous reports [12]. The calculated effective two photon absorption coefficient (β) at different excitation intensity is revealed in Table 1 , which shows that β is weakly dependent on the input fluence. Especially, with the fluence from 55.2 to 108.0mJ/cm2, the β is nearly constant, indicating that a two photon absorption process determines the strong nonlinearity [11]. The imaginary part of the third-order NLO susceptibility (Imχ(3)) can be deduced (β ∼2.1×10−3 cm/GW) to be 8×10−15 esu. A figure of merit (FOM) for the third order optical nonlinearity is defined as FOM=|Imχ(3)/a0|, where a0 is about 2.4cm−1. Therefore, the FOM is determined to be 3.3×10−15 esucm, which is close to that of the MoS2 dispersions (1.0–3.3×10−15 esucm), graphene (5×10−15 esucm) and graphen oxides (4.2×10−15 esucm) [12]. Fig. S1 shows the presence of several oxygen-containing groups around CNDs, which indicates that these CNDs are favorable for further functionalization to enhance the NLO properties for more applications [7,11].

Luminescent CNDs with the mean size of 5.3nm are produced from the bagasse by fs laser ablation in ethanol. These CNDs show excitation wavelength dependent luminescence and MP luminescence. The CNDs without any further functionalization exhibit strong optical limiting response to fs laser pulses with an optical limiting threshold of 74mJ/cm2, which originates from the strong two photon absorption. The present results reveal that CNDs is very promising for application in ultra-fast nonlinear optical micro-devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51072054, 51132004, and 51102209) and the National Basic Research Program of China (2011CB808100). D.Z. Tan wants to thank Nippon Electric Glass Co., Ltd. for the helpful support. Mr. S. Yamamoto and Dr. A. Sakamoto are very appreciated for the help to D.Z. Tan. We would like to thank Dr. T. Takemoto from Osaka Gas Co., Ltd. and Dr. H. Homma from KRI Inc. for preparation of carbonized bagasse.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.12.056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple synthesis of ultra-small nanodiamonds with tunable size and photoluminescence

Synthesis and enhanced nonlinear optical properties of graphene/CdS organic glass

Graphene oxides as tunable broadband nonlinear optical materials for fs laser pulses
